questions.prompt: What defines enterprise computing?

Large-scale computing systems that handle critical business operations for organizations
Personal computing devices used by enterprise employees
Cloud-based software for enterprise social media management

questions.prompt: Which of the following are key characteristics of enterprise computing?
options: High availability and reliability
Single-user access only
Scalability to handle growing workloads
Strong security and data integrity

questions.prompt: What is a mainframe's primary role in enterprise computing?

Processing large volumes of transactions with high reliability and security
Replacing all other servers in the data center
Providing free open-source software to enterprises

questions.prompt: In which industries are mainframes still heavily used today?
options: Banking and finance
Insurance
Retail food trucks
Government and healthcare

questions.prompt: What percentage of Fortune 500 companies still use mainframes?
options: Approximately 25%
Approximately 50%
Over 70%
Less than 10%

Mainframes provide superior transaction processing, security, and reliability for mission-critical workloads

Mainframes and the cloud are converging to reshape data processing and management

The cloud is a subset of mainframe technology

A distinct era in the evolution of computing infrastructure
Identical approaches to data storage and processing
Legacy and modern versions of the same operating system

questions.prompt: What does enterprise computing refer to?
options: Computing done by a single employee on their workstation
The use of computer systems and software within a large organization to handle business processes

Personal computing devices used at home

options: Data storage and processing

A robust and scalable network infrastructure

The resilience of mainframe systems with the dynamic possibilities of cloud computing

questions.prompt: What is the primary benefit of hybrid cloud architectures for enterprises?

Empowering enterprises with efficiency and innovation by bridging traditional and contemporary computing
Eliminating all security requirements

As end users of financial, retail, and data management transactions processed by mainframes

An unavailable transaction could cause a client to permanently switch to a competitor

Divided into two meanings: the hardware (Z-Series chip) and the operating systems supported by it

As a cloud-based service offering

questions.prompt: What makes mainframes resilient according to the page?

Layers of redundancy
Their small physical footprint

options: Modernization on the mainframe is much more cost-effective than complete modernization outside the mainframe
Migrating off the mainframe is always cheaper

10x more

options: There is no correlation between mainframe investment and performance
A direct positive correlation between mainframe investment and overall performance

Mainframe investment only benefits small companies

options: The mainframe is inherently secure with no additional effort needed
The mainframe is the most secure platform only when appropriately managed
The mainframe is less secure than distributed systems by default

Mainframe data stays isolated and is not accessible from other systems
Mainframe security requires no passwords

questions.prompt: What risk does increased mainframe integration and connectivity create?

It challenges the assumption of complete security, potentially leading to organizational complacency and increased risk
It makes the mainframe slower
It reduces the need for security personnel

options: Virtualization
Cloud computing and Big Data

Security

The 1950s
The 1990s

Its ability to process and analyze massive amounts of data

questions.prompt: Why are mainframes ideal for protecting sensitive data?

They are known for their security features

The end of all mainframe production

Seven

questions.prompt: What is Zowe?
options: A proprietary IBM mainframe operating system
An integrated and extensible open source framework for z/OS
A cloud storage service for mainframe data
A replacement for JCL

questions.prompt: What kind of interfaces does Zowe offer for interacting with z/OS?
options: Only command-line interfaces
Modern interfaces similar to what you may experience on cloud platforms
Only legacy 3270 terminal interfaces
No interfaces; it is a backend-only framework

questions.prompt: What can users do with Zowe according to the page?
options: Setup and use the framework
Extend the framework with plug-ins and extensions
Replace the z/OS operating system entirely
Troubleshoot and contribute to the project

options: Direct access storage (DASD)
Network-attached storage (NAS)
USB flash drives
Storage area networks (SAN) and object storage

questions.prompt: What historical concepts do mainframes preserve due to backward compatibility?
options: Floppy disk drives
Tracks and cylinders with ECKD formatting
Magnetic tape only storage
Cloud-native object storage only

questions.prompt: What trade-off does enterprise storage management involve according to the webcast?
options: Speed versus cost of hardware only
Speed versus protection in data strategies
Storage capacity versus physical size
Cloud versus on-premise exclusively

questions.prompt: What terminal emulator is used to connect to TSO/E on z/OS?
options: SSH client
RDP client
Telnet standard

questions.prompt: What is the first interface you access after connecting to a mainframe via TN3270?
options: ISPF directly
TSO/E (Time Sharing Option/Extensions)
UNIX System Services
IMS

questions.prompt: What is ISPF and what does it provide?
options: A programming language for mainframe applications
A menu-driven interface for navigating and managing z/OS components
A database management system
A network monitoring tool

questions.prompt: What can you do through ISPF panels?
options: Edit code and submit jobs
Manage datasets
Browse social media
Monitor system activities

questions.prompt: What is Job Control Language (JCL) used for?
options: Writing business application logic in COBOL
Communicating instructions to IBM mainframe systems to execute tasks
Creating graphical user interfaces
Managing network configurations

questions.prompt: What are the three main sections of every JCL script?
options: JOB Statement, EXEC Statement, and DD Statement
INPUT, PROCESS, and OUTPUT
START, RUN, and END
DEFINE, ALLOCATE, and EXECUTE

questions.prompt: What is the role of an EXEC statement in JCL?
options: It defines the datasets used in the job
It specifies the program or procedure to execute for a job step
It provides accounting information for the job
It sends notifications to the system operator

questions.prompt: How does JCL differ from general-purpose programming languages?
options: JCL is used for defining jobs with sequences of steps, not for business logic
JCL is identical to COBOL in syntax and purpose
JCL is used for web development
JCL can perform complex mathematical calculations on its own

questions.prompt: Where does the JOB statement appear in a JCL script?
options: After every EXEC statement
Always as the first line in a JCL script
At the end of the JCL script
Only in cataloged procedures, not in the main JCL

questions.prompt: Which parameters can be specified in a JOB statement?
options: CLASS (determines priority and resource allocation)
MSGCLASS (specifies where system logs are sent)
DSN (dataset name)
NOTIFY (sends message to submitter upon completion)

questions.prompt: What does the CLASS parameter in a JOB statement do?
options: Specifies the programming language to use
Determines the priority and resource allocation for the job
Defines the dataset class for output
Sets the security classification of the job

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the MSGCLASS parameter?
options: It defines the message format for COBOL programs
It specifies where system output and log messages are sent
It sets the debug level for the job
It determines the number of messages allowed

questions.prompt: What is the primary role of the EXEC statement in JCL?
options: To define datasets used by the job
To specify the program or procedure that a step will execute
To provide accounting information
To end the job

questions.prompt: What does PGM= specify in an EXEC statement?
options: The priority of the program
The name of the program or utility to execute
The programming language used
The page number of the manual

questions.prompt: How many steps can a single JCL job include?
options: Up to 99 steps
Up to 255 steps
Up to 1000 steps
Unlimited steps

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the DD (Data Definition) statement in JCL?
options: To define the job name and accounting information
To define the datasets used in the job and link the program to its resources
To specify the program to execute
To set conditional execution parameters

questions.prompt: What does the DISP parameter in a DD statement control?
options: The display format of the output
How the dataset is handled (created, shared, or deleted)
The disposition of the job in the queue
The display mode for error messages

questions.prompt: Which DISP parameter values are valid for dataset disposition?
options: SHR (shared access to existing dataset)
NEW (creates a new dataset)
DEL (deletes the program)
MOD (appends records without overwriting)

questions.prompt: What does DISP=OLD indicate?
options: The dataset is a backup copy
The dataset already exists and no other job can access it until the current job step completes
The dataset is archived and no longer accessible
The dataset is a temporary work file

questions.prompt: What utility is commonly used in JCL to allocate or delete datasets without doing any processing?
options: IEBGENER
IEFBR14
IEBCOPY
IDCAMS

questions.prompt: What does SPACE=(CYL,(1,1),RLSE) do in a DD statement?
options: Allocates 1 cylinder initially with 1 additional and releases unused space
Specifies the space is on cylinder 1 of the disk
Sets the space to 1% of available storage
Creates 1 megabyte of space with no release option

questions.prompt: In the DCB parameter, what does RECFM=FB specify?
options: The file is in free-block format
Fixed-length records with a specified record length and block size
The file is in variable-block format
The record format is undecided

questions.prompt: What does DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE) mean?
options: Create and catalog if successful, delete if the job fails
Delete the dataset immediately regardless of outcome
Create without cataloging, keep on failure
Catalog an existing dataset

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of JOBLIB in JCL?
options: To define the output library for job results
To specify the library where the system should search for programs used in all steps of the job
To store the JCL source code
To define the system log location

questions.prompt: What does STEPLIB do that differs from JOBLIB?
options: STEPLIB applies only to a specific step, while JOBLIB applies to all steps
STEPLIB is used for datasets, not programs
JOBLIB is used for procedures only
There is no difference; they are interchangeable

questions.prompt: What standard system program library does z/OS search automatically for IBM-supplied programs?
options: SYS1.LINKLIB
SYS1.PARMLIB
SYS1.PROCLIB
SYS1.MACLIB

questions.prompt: When would you need to specify JOBLIB or STEPLIB in your JCL?
options: When running built-in IBM utilities
When the program resides in a private program library
When submitting a job from TSO
When using the MSGCLASS parameter

questions.prompt: What defines the start and end of an instream procedure in JCL?
options: PROC statement marks the start and PEND marks the end
BEGIN marks the start and END marks the end
START marks the start and STOP marks the end
// marks the start and // marks the end

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between an instream procedure and a cataloged procedure?
options: Instream procedures run faster than cataloged procedures
Instream procedures are written directly in the same JCL member where executed; cataloged procedures are stored in a separate dataset
Cataloged procedures cannot use symbolic parameters
Instream procedures can only be used by system programmers

questions.prompt: What statements can be included inside an instream procedure?
options: Only DD and EXEC statements
CNTL, COMMENT, DD, EXEC, IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF, INCLUDE, and SET
Only JOB and DD statements
Only JCL conditional statements

questions.prompt: What is the scope of an instream procedure?
options: It can be used by any job in the system
It is limited to the job in which it is defined
It can be used across all jobs submitted by the same user
It is stored in the system procedure library permanently

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of conditional statements in JCL?
options: To define dataset attributes
To let a job decide which steps to run based on previous step results
To specify the programming language used in the job
To format the output of a program

questions.prompt: What does the IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF construct in JCL check?
options: The availability of disk space
The return code (RC) of previous steps
The current time of day
The username of the job submitter

questions.prompt: In which scenarios are conditional statements in JCL commonly used?
options: Error handling and skipping steps if a previous step failed
Branching logic based on previous results
Replacing the JOB statement
Making jobs more flexible without running everything every time

questions.prompt: What does COND=(0,EQ) tell JCL to do?
options: Run the step only if the previous step's return code was zero
Skip the step if the previous step's return code was zero (no errors)
Execute the step regardless of any return code
Abort the job if the return code is zero

questions.prompt: What does COND=EVEN do in JCL?
options: Runs the step only if all previous steps had a return code of zero
Runs the step even if something above failed or abended
Skips the step if the previous step completed normally
Terminates the job if any step ends with a non-zero code

questions.prompt: In the example COND=(0,EQ), what does EQ stand for?
options: Equal to the specified return code
End of queue
Error quotient
Execute quietly

questions.prompt: What is the IEBCOMPR utility used for?
options: Copying datasets from one volume to another
Comparing PS, PDS, or PDSE datasets
Compressing partitioned datasets to remove unused space
Creating generation data groups

questions.prompt: When are two PS datasets considered equal by IEBCOMPR?
options: When they have the same name
When they contain the same number of records and corresponding records are identical
When they were created on the same date
When they occupy the same amount of disk space

questions.prompt: What return code does IEBCOMPR produce when files are not identical?
options: Return code 0
Return code 4
Return code 8
Return code 16

questions.prompt: What are common use cases for IEBCOMPR?
options: Verifying backups were done correctly
Comparing PDS/PDSE members for identical content
Creating new datasets
Checking data integrity after migration

questions.prompt: What is IEBGENER primarily used for?
options: Comparing datasets for differences
Copying records from sequential datasets or converting between PS and PDS formats
Compressing partitioned datasets
Defining GDG bases

questions.prompt: Which dataset types can IEBGENER use as input?
options: Physical Sequential (PS) datasets
Partitioned Dataset (PDS) members
VSAM datasets
Partitioned Dataset Extended (PDSE) members

questions.prompt: What DD names does IEBGENER use for input and output datasets?
options: SYSUT1 for input and SYSUT2 for output
INPUT for input and OUTPUT for output
SYSIN for input and SYSOUT for output
INDD for input and OUTDD for output

questions.prompt: What is IEBCOPY used for?
options: Comparing sequential datasets
Copying, merging, and compressing Partitioned Datasets (PDS) or PDSE
Generating reports from job output
Defining GDG base entries

questions.prompt: What operations can IEBCOPY perform?
options: Copy an entire PDS/PDSE
Copy selected members from a PDS/PDSE
Compare records between sequential files
Compress a PDS by removing unused space

questions.prompt: What DD names does IEBCOPY use for input and output?
options: SYSUT1 and SYSUT2
INDD and OUTDD (via SYSIN control statements)
INPUT and OUTPUT
SOURCE and TARGET

questions.prompt: What utility is used to define a GDG base?
options: IEFBR14
IDCAMS
IEBGENER
IEBCOPY

questions.prompt: What is the maximum LIMIT value for a GDG?
options: Unlimited

questions.prompt: What happens when EMPTY is specified on a GDG definition and the generation limit is reached?
options: Only the oldest generation is deleted
All existing generations are deleted at once when a new generation is added
The job terminates with an error
The newest generation is deleted to make room

questions.prompt: What does SCRATCH specify in GDG parameters?
options: Generations are kept indefinitely
Generations are physically removed from disk when uncataloged
Only the catalog entry is removed, data stays on disk
Generations are backed up to tape automatically

questions.prompt: What is a GDG base?
options: A physical dataset that stores all generation data
A catalog entry that defines the common name for all generation datasets
A backup of all generation datasets
A JCL procedure for managing datasets

questions.prompt: What utility is required to create a GDG base before using GDG generations?
options: IEFBR14
IDCAMS
IEBGENER
IEBCOMPR

questions.prompt: In the example GDG base MYDATA.BACKUP.REPORT with LIMIT(5) and NOEMPTY, what happens when the 6th generation is created?
options: The job fails with an error
Only the oldest generation is deleted to make room for the new one
All generations are deleted
The newest generation replaces the oldest directly

questions.prompt: What naming convention does the system use for GDG generations?
options: BASE.NAME.Vn (where n is a version number)
BASE.NAME.GxxxxVxx (generation and version numbers)
BASE.NAME.Dyymmdd (date-based naming)
BASE.NAME.SEQn (sequence numbers only)

questions.prompt: How do you reference a new generation dataset in JCL relative to the GDG base?
options: Use the full dataset name including generation number
Use DSN=BASE.NAME(+1) to create the next generation
Use DSN=BASE.NAME(0) for a new generation
Use DSN=BASE.NAME(NEW) to create a new generation

questions.prompt: What utility is used in the example to create a GDG generation?
options: IDCAMS
IEFBR14
IEBGENER
IEBCOPY

questions.prompt: What does (0) refer to when referencing a GDG generation?
options: The oldest generation
The latest (most recently created) generation
A non-existent generation placeholder
The first generation ever created

questions.prompt: If you have generations G0005V00 (latest), G0004V00, and G0003V00, what does (-1) reference?
options: An error because -1 is invalid

questions.prompt: What does the relative number (-2) represent in a GDG?
options: The generation created two days ago
The generation created just before the one before the latest
The second generation ever created
The generation that is two versions ahead of the latest

questions.prompt: What command is used to modify properties of an existing GDG base?
options: MODIFY
ALTER
UPDATE
CHANGE

questions.prompt: Which GDG base attributes can be changed using the ALTER command?
options: LIMIT (maximum number of generations)
EMPTY/NOEMPTY behavior
The GDG base name
SCRATCH/NOSCRATCH

questions.prompt: What happens when you DELETE a GDG base whose generations were created with SCRATCH?
options: Only the GDG base catalog entry is removed; generations remain
The GDG base and all generations are completely removed from both catalog and disk
An error occurs because deletions are not allowed
Only the newest generation is preserved
